html:
Understanding the 3D Projection
Welcome! In this 3D setup, you can see the world axes Xw, Yw, and Zw. Dashed rays pass through the camera center and land on the tilted image plane.
⚠ Figure failed to load

plan.contextChunk:
Simply placing them at evenly spaced intervals along the axis in the
image won't be correct. Due to geometric distortion introduced by
perspective projection, the tick marks are not evenly spaced within the
image plane. This distortion affects each axis in a distinct manner.

Let's start assuming we know the location of some arbitrary 3D distance
to the origin on each axis that we will denote by $\alpha_X$, $\alpha_Y$
and $\alpha_Z$. The question is, where do we place the ticks for
$k\alpha_X$, $k\alpha_Y$ and $k\alpha_Z$ for all $k$? We will use the
cross-ratio to calibrate the projection of the world coordinate system
in the image plane. For doing this we will need the location of the
horizon line of a reference plane (i.e. the ground plane), the position
of the orthogonal vanishing point and the real measure of one object in
the scene.

As shown in @fig-axis_calibration_procedure (a) we can use the cross-ratio
to estimate the location of the points $Y= k \alpha_X$ where $\alpha_X$
is an arbitrary constant as shown in @fig-axis_calibration_procedure (b),
and the corresponding image location $r$, for $k=1$, is chosen
arbitrarily as shown in @fig-axis_calibration_procedure (a). Due to
perspective projection, the image projection of the point
$Y= k \alpha_X$ will not be evenly spaced in the image. In this
particular example we chose the orientation of the world axis in image
coordinates to be aligned with the location of the vanishing points.

![Using the cross-ratio invariant to project the world coordinates into the image using only the vanishing points.](figures/single_view_3d/axis_calibration_procedure.png){width="100%" #fig-axis_calibration_procedure}

In order to calibrate the axis we need to know the dimensions of an
object in the scene. We need measurements along the three axis. In this
example, we used the bookshelf height (197 cm) and the radius of the
table base (50 cm). Using these reference measurements we can solve for
the image locations of the ticks $\alpha_X=\alpha_Y=\alpha_Z=1$ m.

@fig-office_all_calibrated_box shows the final calibrated axes with
ticks placed each 50 cm along each of the three axes.

![Calibrated world axes. Each axis is calibrated using the measurement of a scene element parallel to the world-coordinate axis. In this example, we used the bookshelf height (197 cm) and the radius of the table base (50 cm). From the calibrated axes, you can see that the office is around 250 cm wide.](figures/single_view_3d/office_all_calibrated_box.png){width="100%" #fig-office_all_calibrated_box}

From this calibrated system we can directly read the height of the
camera. The location of the camera coincides with the location of the
horizon line because, in this example, the ground plane is horizontal.

elements: World coordinate origin point O_w
World axes lines: X_w (blue), Y_w (red), Z_w (green) extending into the positive octant
Equally-spaced 3D tick marks along the world axes at 1, 2, 3, 4, and 5 units
Camera projection center (pinhole) represented as a distinct 3D node
Floating semi-transparent rectangular Image Plane positioned between the camera center and the world coordinate system
Projected axes lines on the image plane corresponding to the projections of X_w, Y_w, and Z_w
Projected tick marks on the image plane showing non-uniform, perspective-compressed spacing
Vanishing points v1, v2, v3 marked as vibrant colored dots on the image plane where the projected parallel directions meet
Dashed projection rays connecting world tick marks through the pinhole to their projected locations on the image plane
Horizon line connecting vanishing points v1 and v2 on the image plane

interactions.label: View Mode
interactions.teaches: Switches between a 3D spatial view showing how rays project onto the tilted image plane, and the 2D projected camera view showing the compressed tick marks and vanishing points

interactions.label: Camera Pitch
interactions.teaches: Tilting the camera up and down shifts the horizon line and moves the vertical vanishing point (v3) closer to or further from the image center, drastically altering vertical compression

interactions.label: Focal Length (f)
interactions.teaches: A longer focal length moves the vanishing points outward and reduces perspective distortion, making the projected tick marks appear more evenly spaced

interactions.label: World Unit (alpha)
interactions.teaches: Scales the physical spacing of the ticks in the 3D world, showing that while the absolute positions change, the cross-ratio of any four points remains completely invariant

demo_steps.title: Understanding the 3D Projection
demo_steps.narration: Welcome! In this 3D setup, you can see the world axes X_w, Y_w, and Z_w. Notice how light rays (dashed lines) connect the evenly spaced 3D world ticks through the camera center to project them onto the tilted image plane.

demo_steps.title: The Perspective Compression Illusion
demo_steps.narration: Let's switch to the camera's point-of-view. Look at the projected axes: even though the 3D world ticks are perfectly equidistant, the projected ticks compress closer together as they approach the vanishing points (v1, v2, v3). This is perspective compression.

demo_steps.title: How Camera Tilt Alters the Grid
demo_steps.narration: Tilt the camera upward by adjusting Pitch to -15 degrees. Watch how the vertical vanishing point v3 shifts downward and the vertical Y-axis ticks compress rapidly toward it. The location of the vanishing points dictates the entire coordinate grid layout.

demo_steps.title: Focal Length and Distortion
demo_steps.narration: Now, increase the focal length to 4.5 (zoom in). The vanishing points push far outside the image boundary, and the perspective compression relaxes. The tick spacing looks much more uniform, mimicking orthographic projection.

demo_steps.title: Calibrating with the Cross-Ratio
demo_steps.narration: By changing the world unit 'alpha', we scale the 3D coordinates. Because the cross-ratio of projective geometry is invariant under projection, knowing just one physical measurement allows us to mathematically compute and calibrate all other tick locations along these distorted axes.

interactionPlan.camera_suggestion: Elevated perspective view looking down at the 3D coordinate origin, showing the camera center, the floating image plane, and the world axes in a single balanced composition.
interactionPlan.notes: When 'View Mode' is toggled to 'Camera View', smoothly transition the Three.js camera to match the position and orientation of the defined camera node. Ensure the projected points and vanishing points are dynamically recalculated using basic projective transformation matrices based on the current Pitch and Focal Length values.

attempts.html:
Understanding the 3D Projection
⚠ Figure failed to load

discrepancies: Severe visual clutter due to overlapping labels and ticks, particularly near the origin and along the axes
The 3D view projects all three axes (X, Y, Z) simultaneously with all 5 ticks, whereas the original figure (a) cleanly illustrates only the Y-axis projection to explain the concept
Labels like 'pinhole C' and 'horizon line' are on top of each other, making them difficult to read
The camera viewpoint in 3D Setup mode does not match the clean, clear tilt of diagram (a)
evaluation.notes: Excellent mathematical implementation of the perspective projection and interactive camera modes, but severely compromised by extreme label clutter and overlapping elements.
action_items: Implement a decluttering mechanism for labels, such as dynamically hiding tick labels when they are too close to each other or when in 3D view mode
Limit the projection rays to only one axis (e.g., the Y-axis) by default to match the clarity of diagram (a), with interactive toggles to show other axes
Adjust the initial camera angle in 3D Setup mode to better mimic the clear, angled perspective shown in diagram (a)
Increase the font size of key labels (like O, C, v1, v2) while reducing the size or density of the tick mark labels to establish a clear visual hierarchy

feedback.rationale: The mathematical concept and interactivity are highly accurate, but the rendering suffers from severe label clutter, camera alignment issues, and default visibility settings that require concrete implementation refinements by the generator.
action_items: Implement a decluttering mechanism for labels, such as dynamically hiding tick labels when they are too close to each other or when in 3D view mode
Limit the projection rays to only one axis (e.g., the Y-axis) by default to match the clarity of diagram (a), with interactive toggles to show other axes
Adjust the initial camera angle in 3D Setup mode to better mimic the clear, angled perspective shown in diagram (a)
Increase the font size of key labels (like O, C, v1, v2) while reducing the size or density of the tick mark labels to establish a clear visual hierarchy

Increase the font size of key labels (like O, C, v1, v2) while reducing the size or density of the tick mark labels to establish a clear visual hierarchy

attempts.html:
Understanding the 3D Projection
Evenly spaced 3D ticks project through the pinhole onto a tilted image plane.
⚠ Figure failed to load

discrepancies: The 3D Setup view does not clearly separate the tilted image plane and world coordinate axes side-by-side as shown in Figure (a)
Vanishing point labels (v1, v2, v3) are extremely tiny and hard to read compared to the prominent colored spheres
Labels like 'World coordinates' and 'Image plane' are placed too high and overlap with the top UI controls
The tick labels on the projected axes are extremely small and cluttered near the center
evaluation.notes: Excellent interactive functionality and educational step-throughs, but suffers from poor visual scaling, tiny labels, and a cluttered 3D camera viewpoint compared to the clean layout of the source figures.
action_items: Increase the size of all axis labels (Xw, Yw, Zw) and vanishing point labels (v1, v2, v3) to make them clearly legible

Adjust the initial camera position in '3D Setup' mode to better replicate the perspective of Figure (a), showing the clear separation between the tilted image plane on the left and the world coordinate axis on the right
Adjust the layout/margins of the UI panels so they do not overlap with the 3D scene labels at the top
Increase the size and contrast of the ticks and tick label annotations (e.g., alpha, 2alpha) on the projected axes

feedback.rationale: The interactive functionality and concept are solid, but concrete visual execution issues such as tiny labels, cluttered tick sizes, overlapping UI panels, and sub-optimal camera angles need to be refined by the generator.
action_items: Increase the size of all axis labels (Xw, Yw, Zw) and vanishing point labels (v1, v2, v3) to make them clearly legible

Adjust the initial camera position in '3D Setup' mode to better replicate the perspective of Figure (a), showing the clear separation between the tilted image plane on the left and the world coordinate axis on the right
Adjust the layout/margins of the UI panels so they do not overlap with the 3D scene labels at the top
Increase the size and contrast of the ticks and tick label annotations (e.g., alpha, 2alpha) on the projected axes

Adjust the layout/margins of the UI panels so they do not overlap with the 3D scene labels at the top
Increase the size and contrast of the ticks and tick label annotations (e.g., alpha, 2alpha) on the projected axes

attempts.html:
Understanding the 3D Projection
Welcome! In this 3D setup, you can see the world axes Xw, Yw, and Zw. Dashed rays pass through the camera center and land on the tilted image plane.
⚠ Figure failed to load

discrepancies: The camera projection center is shown as a large purple sphere in 3D Setup, which is not depicted as such in the original figure
The original diagram (a) specifically labels points P1, P2, P3, P4 on the Yw axis, which are represented as generic ticks in the 3D view
The image plane in the original figure has a solid white background with a black border, whereas the 3D model uses a semi-transparent blue fill
In 3D Setup view, some labels can overlap or align awkwardly depending on the camera angle
evaluation.notes: Excellent interactive reproduction of camera projection geometry with high quality step-through explanations and realistic 3D/2D transitions.
action_items: Add specific labels for P2, P3, P4 on the Y_w axis in the 3D setup view to match figure (a) more closely
Make the image plane background white with slight opacity instead of blue to match the original paper aesthetic
Adjust the camera center C visual representation to be more subtle (e.g., a smaller dot or simple coordinate frame) rather than a solid purple sphere
Fine-tune the label placement offsets to prevent minor overlaps during transitions

feedback.rationale: The figure is of very high quality with an overall average score of 4.4, outstanding interactivity and concept accuracy, and only minor cosmetic suggestions remaining.
